# **Comprehensive Analysis of Khata Transfer Regulations and Procedures in Bangalore (2025-2026)**

In the administrative and legal architecture of Karnataka’s real estate sector, the term "Khata" serves as the primary instrument for municipal revenue accounting and tax assessment within the jurisdiction of the Bruhat Bengaluru Mahanagara Palike (BBMP). Derived from Urdu and Hindi roots meaning "account," a Khata represents the official entry in the municipal property assessment register.1 While the sale deed and registration under the Registration Act, 1908, establish legal title between individuals, the Khata transfer serves as the essential mutation process that recognizes this ownership change at the civic governance level.3 This report provides an exhaustive reference for legal practitioners, property owners, and researchers on the mechanisms of Khata transfer in Bangalore, reflecting the significant shift toward digital governance via the e-Aasthi portal and the transition from the Karnataka Municipal Corporations (KMC) Act, 1976, to the independent Bruhat Bengaluru Mahanagara Palike Act, 2020\.6

## **1\. Legal Definition and Structural Framework of Khata Transfer**

A Khata is fundamentally a municipal record that identifies the person primarily liable for the payment of property taxes on a specific parcel of land or building.2 It is neither a document of title nor a proof of ownership in a court of law; however, it remains the most vital administrative document after the sale deed.2 The process of Khata transfer, or "mutation," is triggered whenever the ownership of a property changes hands, necessitating an update in the municipal records to ensure tax assessment and civic service delivery are directed to the correct individual.3

### **Triggers for Khata Transfer**

The necessity for a Khata transfer arises under specific legal and life-cycle events involving immovable property. These triggers are codified under the BBMP Act 2020 and are essential for maintaining the integrity of the urban tax base.3

| Event Trigger | Description | Legal Basis |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Property Sale** | The execution and registration of a sale deed between a buyer and a seller.3 | Section 149, BBMP Act 2020 11 |
| **Inheritance** | The death of the original Khatedar (account holder), leading to the transfer to legal heirs.4 | Intestate or Testate Succession 12 |
| **Gift Deed** | Voluntary transfer of property ownership without monetary consideration.4 | Transfer of Property Act 15 |
| **Court Decree** | Transfer of title mandated by a judicial order or legal settlement.3 | Judicial Adjudication 12 |
| **Partition/Release** | Division of ancestral or joint property among family members.3 | Family Settlement 2 |

### **Legal Effect on Ownership Records**

While a Khata does not confer title, its legal effect is profound regarding the "Right of Entry" into the municipal ecosystem.9 It acts as *prima facie* evidence of possession and tax liability.16 Without a Khata transfer, the new owner is legally invisible to the BBMP, which prevents the issuance of building plan approvals, trade licenses, and the official assessment of property taxes in the new owner's name.1 Furthermore, modern digital systems like Kaveri 2.0 and e-Aasthi have integrated these records so deeply that a property without an updated e-Khata is effectively "frozen" in the secondary market, as future registrations will be blocked.3

## **2\. Distinction Between Property Registration and Khata Transfer**

In Karnataka, the bifurcation of property title (Registration) and property tax accounting (Khata) is a foundational principle. Professionals must distinguish between these two layers to ensure comprehensive legal compliance.5

### **Property Registration: The Title Layer**

Registration is the formal process of recording the transfer of interest in immovable property at the Sub-Registrar’s Office (SRO).5 It is governed by the Registration Act, 1908, and involves the payment of stamp duty and registration fees to the state government.16 The primary purpose of registration is to provide public notice of the transaction and to establish the legal validity of the title deed.5 Once registered, the buyer owns the property under the Transfer of Property Act, 1882, but they have not yet entered the municipal revenue net.3

### **Khata Transfer: The Revenue Layer**

Khata transfer is the subsequent administrative step of notifying the BBMP of the registered title change.3 It is governed by the BBMP Act, 2020 (formerly the KMC Act, 1976).6 The objective is to update the Property Assessment Register to reflect the current taxpayer.2 While registration is a one-time event conducted at the SRO, Khata is a living record maintained by the BBMP Revenue Department.2

### **Necessity of Dual Compliance**

The separation exists because the SRO does not verify the legality of a building’s construction or its compliance with municipal bylaws; its only concern is the legality of the deed itself.5 Conversely, the BBMP uses the Khata transfer process to audit the property’s compliance with zoning laws, building permits, and the payment of betterment charges.1 This dual system prevents the state from inadvertently validating illegal constructions while ensuring that even "unauthorized" properties (B-Khata) are brought into the tax net under Section 144 of the BBMP Act.19

## **3\. Governing Body and Official Portal Ecosystem**

The Bruhat Bengaluru Mahanagara Palike (BBMP) is the apex municipal authority for Bangalore, managing property tax and Khata for approximately 25 lakh properties.1 The Revenue Department within the BBMP, led by the Special Commissioner (Revenue) and supported by Zonal Commissioners, oversees the ARO (Assistant Revenue Officer) offices in individual wards where Khata applications are processed.18

### **Official Portals and Digital Infrastructure**

The governance of Khata has undergone an aggressive digital transformation between 2024 and 2026\. The manual handwritten registers at ward offices have been superseded by centralized digital platforms.18

* **e-Aasthi Portal (Primary):** [https://bbmpeaasthi.karnataka.gov.in](https://bbmpeaasthi.karnataka.gov.in) This portal is the current definitive source for "e-Khata" (digital Khata).18 It allows property owners to search for "Draft e-Khatas," verify property details through geo-tagging, and apply for the "Final e-Khata".18  
* **Sakala Online Services:** [https://sakala.kar.nic.in](https://sakala.kar.nic.in) Used for time-bound Khata transfer applications under the Suvarna Khatha scheme.4  
* **BBMP Property Tax Portal:** [https://bbmptax.karnataka.gov.in](https://bbmptax.karnataka.gov.in) Essential for checking tax arrears, paying property tax, and resolving discrepancies in tax receipts before initiating a transfer.24  
* **Seva Sindhu:** [https://sevasindhu.karnataka.gov.in](https://sevasindhu.karnataka.gov.in) A unified service portal often used for obtaining Legal Heir Certificates and other ancillary documents required for inheritance-based transfers.19

## **4\. Step-by-Step Official Transfer Processes**

The procedural requirements for Khata transfer have been standardized through the e-Aasthi framework, yet the documentary burden and verification depth vary across scenarios.18

### **(a) Transfer After Property Sale**

The sale-based transfer is the standard workflow for resale properties and newly constructed units handed over by developers.3

1. **Preparation and Tax Clearance:** The applicant must first ensure that all property tax dues are paid up to date.3 The system will block any transfer application if the SAS (Self-Assessment Scheme) record shows pending payments or interest.3  
2. **Draft e-Khata Search:** Property owners log into the e-Aasthi portal and search for their property using the PID (Property ID) or the Registered Deed Number from Kaveri.18 If a "Draft e-Khata" is present, the details must be verified against the physical sale deed.21  
3. **Data Enrichment and Document Upload:** The buyer must upload high-quality scans of the registered sale deed, the mother deed (previous chain of title), and the latest Encumbrance Certificate (EC) for at least 13-30 years.4  
4. **Geo-Tagging and Photography:** A unique requirement of the e-Aasthi system is the uploading of a current photograph of the property and the confirmation of its location on a GPS-enabled map.18  
5. **Aadhaar e-KYC:** The applicant performs an OTP-based identity verification.18 For NRIs, passport-based verification is available through manual ARO intervention.18  
6. **Mutation and Objection Period:** Once submitted, the BBMP issues a digital acknowledgment. A 7-day public notice is often generated online for any objections to be filed.18  
7. **Field Inspection:** For properties with significant deviations or in specific zones, the ARO may conduct a physical field inspection to verify building measurements against the sanctioned plan.3  
8. **Fee Payment and Final Issuance:** Upon ARO approval, the applicant pays the 2% transfer fee online and can then download the digital Final e-Khata Certificate and Extract.4

### **(b) Transfer After Death/Inheritance (Succession Khata)**

Inheritance transfers require establishing the legal hierarchy of claimants to ensure the BBMP does not inadvertently facilitate property disputes.4

1. **Legal Heirship Documentation:** The heirs must first apply for a Family Tree (Vamsha Vriksha) or a Legal Heir Certificate from the Revenue Department/Tahsildar.4  
2. **Death Certificate Integration:** The registered death certificate of the deceased Khatedar is a mandatory upload.4  
3. **Affidavit of Consent:** If there are multiple heirs (e.g., a surviving spouse and three children), but only one heir is to hold the Khata, notarized No Objection Certificates (NOCs) and affidavits from the other heirs must be submitted.4  
4. **Public Notice Period:** Inheritance-based transfers trigger a mandatory 30-day public notice period (often longer than the 7-day sale notice) to allow any unknown claimants or creditors to object.18  
5. **Administrative Approval:** The ARO manually scrutinizes the family tree and the death certificate against the existing property records before approving the mutation.27

### **(c) Transfer After Gift Deed**

Gift transfers are common for internal family estate planning and follow a hybrid process of sales and inheritance.12

1. **Registered Gift Deed:** The transfer can only be initiated after the Gift Deed is registered at the SRO.12  
2. **Relationship Verification:** While the Khata process is the same, the BBMP verifies if the transfer is within the family to ensure no evasion of betterment charges or other local levies.15  
3. **Application and Aadhar Linkage:** The donee (receiver) completes the e-Aasthi workflow, linking their Aadhaar and capturing the property’s current geo-location.18  
4. **Process Parity:** The fees and timelines are largely identical to the sale scenario, though the 7-day public notice is standard.14

## **5\. Complete Document Checklist Comparison**

The documentary requirements are the primary filter through which the BBMP ensures the "chain of title" is unbroken. Errors here are the leading cause of the 1.4 lakh application backlog reported in 2025\.28

| Category | Sale Transfer | Inheritance Transfer | Gift Transfer |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Primary Deed** | Registered Sale Deed.3 | Registered Will or Intestate Proof.4 | Registered Gift Deed.4 |
| **Tax Proof** | Latest Property Tax Paid Receipts.3 | All pending taxes cleared up to death date.37 | Latest Property Tax Paid Receipts.12 |
| **Title History** | Mother Deed / Previous Title Deed.4 | Succession Certificate / Family Tree.4 | Previous Owner's Khata.12 |
| **Encumbrance** | EC (Form 15\) for last 13-30 years.3 | EC showing no pending bank mortgages.13 | EC for 13+ years.14 |
| **Status Documents** | OC/CC and Sanctioned Plan (for A-Khata).4 | Death Certificate of the owner.4 | ID Proof of Donor and Donee.14 |
| **Legal/Indemnity** | Self-declaration form.13 | Affidavit & Indemnity Bond.13 | Affidavit for Gift.12 |

## **6\. Fee Structure and Financial Obligations**

Khata transfer is not merely an administrative process but a revenue-generating mechanism for the BBMP. The fee structure is designed to cover administrative costs and infrastructure development.3

### **The 2% Transfer Fee Calculation**

The primary cost of a Khata transfer is a standard fee of 2% of the stamp duty paid at the time of property registration.4

* **Basis of Calculation:** ![][image1]  
* **Example Case:** A property with a guidance value of ![][image2] attracts a 5% stamp duty (![][image3]). The Khata transfer fee is ![][image4].42

### **Betterment Charges (Improvement Charges)**

Betterment charges are one-time levies imposed on properties in newly added wards or those being regularized from B-Khata to A-Khata.22 The rationale is that urban infrastructure improvements (roads, drains, streetlights) increase property value, and owners must contribute to these capital costs.22

| Property Type | Betterment Rate (Guidance Value %) | Minimum Rate (per Sq. Meter) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Residential** | 0.5%.22 | ![][image5].22 |
| **Industrial** | 1.0%.22 | ![][image6].22 |
| **Commercial** | 1.5%.22 | ![][image7].22 |
| **Other** | 0.5%.22 | ![][image5].22 |

**Penalty for Non-Payment:** Failure to pay betterment charges when levied results in an interest rate of 1.5% per month (18% annually).22 For instance, a charge of ![][image8] left unpaid for 10 years would result in a total liability of ![][image9] due to compounded interest.22

### **Mandatory Tax Clearance**

A Khata transfer will be summarily rejected if any property tax is pending.3 This includes the current year's tax and any historical arrears detected during the digitization of records.3 In 2024, the BBMP launched a "Tax-First, Khata-Later" scheme allowing owners to obtain a temporary tax number to pay dues before their Khata is finalized.24

## **7\. Official Processing Timelines: SLA vs. Reality**

The Karnataka Sakala Services Act, 2011, mandates strictly defined timelines for municipal services to reduce corruption and administrative lethargy.27

### **Official Service Level Agreements (SLAs)**

* **Final e-Khata Issuance:** 7 Working Days from submission of a complete application.18  
* **New Khata Registration:** 60 Working Days.27  
* **Khata Transfer (Resale/Gift):** 30 Working Days.4  
* **Correction/Dispute Resolution:** 30 Working Days.27

### **Real-World Constraints in 2025-2026**

Despite the 48-hour "instant e-Khata" promise for simple cases, several factors cause significant delays 27:

1. **System Rigidities:** The e-Aasthi software's rigid data-matching protocols often flag minor name variations, routing applications to manual ARO review.52  
2. **Backlogs:** High transaction volumes in zones like Mahadevapura and Yelahanka have extended processing times to 45-90 days for complex mutations.28  
3. **Faceless vs. Physical:** While the system is designed to be faceless, approximately 20% of applications still require a physical site inspection or manual verification of the "Mother Deed" chain at the ward office.3

## **8\. Legal Provisions and Legislative Framework**

The governing law for property administration in Bangalore transitioned significantly with the passage of the Bruhat Bengaluru Mahanagara Palike Act, 2020, which replaced the KMC Act, 1976, for the city.6

### **Key Sections of the BBMP Act, 2020**

* **Section 144 (Tax Assessment):** Establishes the SAS (Self-Assessment Scheme) and the power to collect tax from unauthorized buildings.19  
* **Section 149 (Obligation to Give Notice of Transfer):**  
  * **Three-Month Rule:** Both transferor and transferee must notify the Commissioner within **three months** of a property sale or registration.2  
  * **One-Year Rule for Death:** Heirs must notify the Commissioner within **one year** of the death of the Khatedar.11  
  * **Penalty for Neglect:** Failure to give notice makes the transferor (seller) continuously liable for all future property taxes until the record is updated.11  
* **Section 150 (Review of Transfer):** Grants the Commissioner the power to reopen any Khata transfer case within **three years** if it is found to have been obtained via fraud, misrepresentation, or suppression of material facts.11  
* **Section 114A (Legacy Clause):** Carried over from the KMC Act, allowing for the cancellation of erroneous entries after a formal inquiry.16

### **Critical Government Orders (GOs)**

* **B-to-A Conversion Order (2024):** A landmark order allowing for the regularization of qualifying B-Khata properties registered before September 30, 2024, upon payment of a 5% Guidance Value penalty and betterment charges.23  
* **Mandatory e-Khata Order (Oct 2024):** A state directive making e-Khata mandatory for all secondary market registrations to curb double-sales and fraudulent titles.19

## **9\. Common Rejection Reasons and Resolution Strategies**

The transition to a digital-first system has introduced technical hurdles that often lead to the rejection of otherwise valid applications.38

| Rejection Ground | Detailed Cause | Corrective Action |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Name Discrepancy** | Initials vs. Full Name (e.g., "S. Kumar" in Aadhaar vs. "Sandeep Kumar" in Deed).39 | Use the "Add Owner" feature in e-Aasthi; submit an affidavit clarifying the name alias to the ARO.39 |
| **Incorrect PID** | Clerical errors during manual entry into the legacy database led to non-existent PIDs.54 | File a manual grievance with previous tax receipts as proof of the historical "Assessment Number".54 |
| **Geo-Tagging Errors** | Photos taken from a distance or GPS coordinates falling on a road/neighboring plot.29 | Re-upload a clear front-view photo with the GPS metadata enabled on a mobile device.29 |
| **Joint Owner Omission** | The system automatically picks only the first name listed in the Kaveri data.52 | Manually add co-owners using their Aadhaar details during the "Draft Verification" stage of the portal.52 |
| **Unapproved Deviations** | Building plan violations exceeding the permissible limit for A-Khata.17 | Re-apply under the B-Khata category or seek regularization under the Akrama-Sakrama scheme (if active).23 |

## **10\. Legal Consequences of Non-Completion of Khata Transfer**

Property owners who treat Khata transfer as an optional formality face severe legal and financial vulnerabilities in the current regulatory climate.3

### **Continued Liability for the Seller**

Under Section 149 of the BBMP Act, the seller remains the "person primarily liable" for property tax until the Khata is transferred.11 If the buyer fails to pay taxes, the BBMP can legally attach the seller’s other assets or accounts to recover the dues.11

### **Frozen Marketability and Liquidity**

The integration of e-Aasthi with Kaveri 2.0 means that a property without an updated e-Khata in the name of the *current owner* cannot be legally resold.3 Registration of a new sale deed will be blocked at the SRO level until the revenue mutation is resolved.3

### **Institutional Financing Bar**

Nationalized banks and housing finance companies (HFCs) now conduct real-time verification of e-Khata status.3 If the Khata is still in the previous owner's name, home loans and loans against property (LAP) will be denied, as the title is considered administratively "clouded".3

### **Denial of Building and Utility Permissions**

The BBMP will not issue building plan approvals for renovations or extensions to owners who do not have an A-Khata in their name.1 Furthermore, utility providers like BESCOM (Electricity) and BWSSB (Water) increasingly require the e-Khata Certificate as a prerequisite for new or transferred service connections.1

## **11\. Conclusions and Strategic Recommendations**

The Khata transfer process in Bangalore has evolved from a passive ward-level entry into a central pillar of digital property governance. For a legal knowledge base, it is essential to emphasize that the Khata is the "Administrative Title" that complements the "Legal Title" of the Sale Deed. Property owners are advised to initiate the transfer within 90 days of registration to avoid the penalties and continuous liability clauses of Section 149 of the BBMP Act 2020\. As the city moves toward a unified digital property record system, ensuring that names, dimensions, and tax histories match across the Kaveri and e-Aasthi databases is the only path to maintaining a truly marketable and legally secure real estate asset in Bangalore.
